coredata.dc:title: A novel enzyme-assisted ultrasonic approach for highly efficient extraction of resveratrol from Polygonum cuspidatum
coredata.dc:creator: Lin, Jer-An
Kuo, Chia-Hung
Chen, Bao-Yuan
Li, Ying
Liu, Yung-Chuan
Chen, Jiann-Hwa
Shieh, Chwen-Jen
coredata.dc:description: Resveratrol is a promising multi-biofunctional phytochemical, which is abundant in Polygonum cuspidatum. Several methods for resveratrol extraction have been reported, while they often take a long extraction time accompanying with poor extraction yield. In this study, a novel enzyme-assisted ultrasonic approach for highly efficient extraction of resveratrol from P. cuspidatum was developed. According to results, the resveratrol yield significantly increased after glycosidases (Pectinex® or Viscozyme®) were applied in the process of extraction, and better extraction efficacy was found in the Pectinex®-assisted extraction compared to Viscozyme®-assisted extraction. Following, a 5-level-4-factor central composite rotatable design with response surface methodology (RSM) and artificial neural network (ANN) was selected to model and optimize the Pectinex®-assisted ultrasonic extraction. Based on the coefficient of determination (R 2) calculated from the design data, ANN model displayed much more accurate in data fitting as compared to RSM model. The optimum conditions for the extraction determined by ANN model were substrate concentration of 5%, acoustic power of 150W, pH of 5.4, temperature of 55°C, the ratio of enzyme to substrate of 3950 polygalacturonase units (PGNU)/g of P. cuspidatum, and reaction time of 5h, which can lead to a significantly high resveratrol yield of 11.88mg/g.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Resveratrol
Polygonum cuspidatum
Enzyme-assisted ultrasonic approach
Response surface methodology
Artificial neural network

HPLC analysis of products extracted from P. cuspidatum
Comparison of predictive capability of RSM and ANN
Efficacy of different enzymes on extraction yield of resveratrol
Comparison of models developed by RSM and ANN

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: HPLC chromatogram of piceid and resveratrol extracted from P. cuspidatum in the (A) absence or (B) presence of enzyme. (C) Effect of Pectinex® and Viscozyme® on the extraction yield of piceid and resveratrol in P. cuspidatum.

Response surface plot showing the relationships between resveratrol yield and reaction parameters: (A) reaction time and reaction temperature; (B) reaction time and enzyme amount; (C) reaction time and pH value. ANN condition.
Evaluation of the predictive capacity of RSM- and ANN-modeling systems.
Coding of experimental parameters and related levels.
Central composite rotatable design and experimental data for 5-level-4-factor response surface analysis.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: AAD: absolute average deviation (%); RMSE: root mean square error; R 2: coefficient of correlation determination.

head.ce:title: A novel enzyme-assisted ultrasonic approach for highly efficient extraction of resveratrol from Polygonum cuspidatum

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Biotechnology Center, National Chung Hsing University, 250 Kuo-kuang Road, Taichung 40227, Taiwan

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Seafood Science, National Kaohsiung Marine University, 142 Haijhuan Road, Nanzih District, Kaohsiung 81157, Taiwan

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Graduate Institute of Molecular Biology, National Chung Hsing University, 250 Kuo-kuang Road, Taichung 40227, Taiwan

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemical Engineering, National Chung Hsing University, 250 Kuo-Kuang Road, Taichung 40227, Taiwan

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Resveratrol is a promising multi-biofunctional phytochemical, which is abundant in Polygonum cuspidatum. Several methods for resveratrol extraction have been reported, while they often take a long extraction time accompanying with poor extraction yield. In this study, a novel enzyme-assisted ultrasonic approach for highly efficient extraction of resveratrol from P. cuspidatum was developed. According to results, the resveratrol yield significantly increased after glycosidases (Pectinex® or Viscozyme®) were applied in the process of extraction, and better extraction efficacy was found in the Pectinex®-assisted extraction compared to Viscozyme®-assisted extraction. Following, a 5-level-4-factor central composite rotatable design with response surface methodology (RSM) and artificial neural network (ANN) was selected to model and optimize the Pectinex®-assisted ultrasonic extraction. Based on the coefficient of determination (R 2) calculated from the design data, ANN model displayed much more accurate in data fitting as compared to RSM model. The optimum conditions for the extraction determined by ANN model were substrate concentration of 5%, acoustic power of 150W, pH of 5.4, temperature of 55°C, the ratio of enzyme to substrate of 3950 polygalacturonase units (PGNU)/g of P. cuspidatum, and reaction time of 5h, which can lead to a significantly high resveratrol yield of 11.88mg/g.

ce:section.ce:para: Polygonum cuspidatum, also called Japanese knotweed, belongs to the Polygonaceae family and is a perennial herb widely distributed in China, Japan, Korea and also found growing widely in America. In ancient Chinese and Japanese herbal medicine, the roots of P. cuspidatum were used in treatments for arthralgia, chronic bronchitis, jaundice, hypertension, and hypercholesterolemia [1]. These plants produce many secondary metabolites with medical value including resveratrol and its derivatives.

Resveratrol (trans-3,4′,5-trihydroxystilbene), a polyphenolic natural product found in grapes, red wine, berries, peanuts, and P. cuspidatum, is biosynthesized in response injury, UV irradiation and fungal attack [2,3]. Resveratrol exhibits a wide variety of pharmacological activities including antioxidative, anticarcinogenic, and antitumor activities [4,5]. Study has also shown that resveratrol exhibits anti-inflammatory activity through modulation of inflammation-associated transcription factor such as nuclear factor-κB (NF-κB) and activator protein-1 (AP-1) [6]. In recent years, resveratrol has become a candidate nutritional substance for cancer prevention widely available as a botanical dietary supplement. Notably, the major source of resveratrol for commercial use is P. cuspidatum roots [7].

In conventional methods, resveratrol is extracted from P. cuspidatum by heating under reflux with solvent, followed by filtration, concentration and purification. However, this procedure is time exhausting and requires a large amount of solvent [8]. Recently, enzyme-assisted extraction methods have been reported for its potential as alternative to conventional solvent based extraction methods [9]. This method has been reported for extraction of vanillin [10], carotenoids [11], and flavonoids [12]. However, the feasibility of using cell wall-degrading enzyme for the extraction of resveratrol from P. cuspidatum roots has not yet been explored.

The influences in the enzyme-assisted extraction are including enzyme amount, extraction duration, temperature, solvent concentration [13]. Mathematical modeling is a valid strategy to inspect the influences of different factors on extraction and to seek the optimal conditions. In statistical-based approaches, response surface methodology (RSM) has been widely applied to optimize the extraction process [14,15]. It is a statistically designed experimental protocol which contrasts with full factorial design for building a second order model. Recently, artificial neural network (ANN) has been developed for modeling the biotechnological process as alternative to RSM system [16,17]. ANN could easily model nonlinear relationships between the dependent and the independent variables without requiring to any prior knowledge of these relationships [18]. These properties make ANN as a powerful tool with higher flexibility in data fitting, prediction, and modeling of biochemical processes compared to the RSM [19].

The aim of present study was to develop an enzyme-assisted ultrasonic approach for highly efficient extraction of resveratrol from P. cuspidatum. The process of extraction was modeled and optimized by RSM and ANN models. Also, the capabilities of RSM and ANN in prediction of data were statistically compared by the coefficient of correlation determination (R 2), absolute average deviation (AAD) and root mean square error (RMSE).

Dried P. cuspidatum roots were purchased from local Chinese herbal medicine stores and ground into powder. The powder was screened with a sieve and particle sizes, between 30 and 45 mesh, were used for the experiments (∼0.62mm). Pectinex® (Pectinex Ultra Clear; 7900PGNU/mL, polygalacturonase unit (PGNU) as defined by Novozymes) and Viscozyme® (Viscozyme L; 100FBG/mL) were purchased from Novo Nordisk Bioindustrials. Resveratrol was purchased from Changsha Nutramax Biotechnology (Changsha, China). Unless otherwise noted, all reagents and chemicals were of analytic grade.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Enzyme-assisted extraction of resveratrol from P. cuspidatum
ce:section.ce:para: The powder ground from dried P. cuspidatum passed through a sieve of 20–40 mesh was prepared for experiments. Powdered P. cuspidatum roots (0.1g) were mixed with enzymes then adding PBS buffer to the final volume of 2mL in a cap-sealed glass tube. The mixture in cell wall-degrading reaction was placed in an orbital sharing bath (150rpm) at designed reaction temperature and time, and then termination with addition of 3mL EtOH. The mixture was then subjected to extraction for resveratrol in a temperature-controlled ultrasonic bath (40kHz, Delta DC150H, Taiwan) set at 70°C and 150W of ultrasonic power for 30min. In the previous study, we compared the efficiency between traditional shaking method (at 100rpm) and ultrasonic method (at 150W) on resveratrol extraction from P. cuspidatum, and the result showed that ultrasonic-assisted extraction possessed higher mass transfer efficacies and extraction yields for resveratrol as compared with conventional shaking extraction [20]. It has been reported that higher acoustic power may lead to degradation of some minor components [21,22]. However, the extraction of resveratrol from P. cuspidatum under ultrasonic power of 150W (1.98W/cm2) did not decrease the yield when compared to the moderate ultrasonic power (90–120W) [23]. Therefore, we employ the ultrasonic extraction condition of 150W. After extraction, insoluble materials were removed by centrifugation at 13,000rpm for 10min. The supernatant was collected and stored in a dark room at 4°C until analysis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: HPLC analysis of products extracted from P. cuspidatum
ce:section.ce:para: The samples were withdrawn from the reaction mixture and analyzed by high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC). Analysis was done by 20μL of the reaction mixture into a HPLC (Hitachi L-7400; Tokyo, Japan) and a Thermo C18 column (250cm 4.6mm, Agilent, USA) was used. Separations were carried out using a gradient elution by employing 10–100% methanol. The reaction mixture separation was achieved by an elution system consisting of 10% methanol for the 15min. Secondary separation was achieved by an elution system consisting of 50% methanol for the 10min. The final analysis was achieved by an elution system consisting of 100% methanol for the 10min. The flow rate was 1.0mL/min and sampling was detected under UV light at 303nm. The products were calculated from the peak areas of the corresponded signals in the chromatogram (Fig.1 A and B).

A four-factor, five-level central composite rotatable design (CCRD) consisting of 27 treatments was employed in this study. To avoid the effect of stumbling factors on the extraction yield, all the experimental runs were performed in a random order. The manipulated (independent) variables and their respective levels selected for enzyme-assisted extraction of resveratrol included temperature (35–75°C), reaction time (1–9h), enzyme amount (79–711PGNU) and pH value (3.0–9.0), which were coded as shown in Table 1 . Table 2 shows the independent factors (xi), levels and experimental design. All reactions were carried out in duplicate.

The optimization results of enzyme assisted extraction of resveratrol using RSM with some extensions in experiments and statistical analysis were used in this study. In the RSM method, the data obtained from the central composite design was fitted into the quadratic polynomial model by regression analysis. The quadratic polynomial model (Eq. (1)) for each response was as follows:
(1)
Y =
β
0
+
β
1
X
1
+
β
2
X
2
+
β
3
X
3
+
β
4
X
4
+
β
12
X
1
X
2
+
β
13
X
1
X
3
+
β
14
X
1
X
4
+
β
23
X
2
X
3
+
β
24
X
2
X

+
β
34
X
3
X
4
+
β
11
X
1

+
β
22
X

33

+
β
44
X
4
2
where Y represents the response variable and β 0 is the interception coefficient. β 1, β 2, β 3 and β 4 are coefficients of the linear effects, β 11, β 22, β 33 and β 44 are coefficients of quadratic effects and β 12, β 13, β 14, β 23, β 24 and β 34 are coefficients of interaction effects for the four independent variables (X 1 =temperature, X 2 =reaction time, X 3 =enzyme amount, X 4 =pH value).

A multi-layer perceptron (MLP) based feed-forward ANN was applied for modeling ultrasound-assisted extraction after Pectinex® treatment in this study. The intelligent problem solver in Neural Power software version 2.5 was used to construct the regression-based networks from the data. Basically, the developed ANN models were composed of an input layer, a hidden layer and an output layer. In present study, one hidden layer was chosen as it is efficient for continuous nonlinear modeling in most cases; otherwise more hidden layers may result in an over-fitting problem. The input layer of proposed ANN had four nodes (neurons), which represented the aforementioned four experimental factors, namely extraction temperature, extraction time, enzyme amount and pH value. The output layer had one neuron, which represented the extraction yield of resveratrol. In this ANN model, the neuron number of hidden layer, the learning algorithm, the transfer functions of hidden layer and output layer, and the iteration were optimally determined, and the best ANN model was finally established.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparison of predictive capability of RSM and ANN
ce:section.ce:para:
Several statistical parameters including the coefficient of determination (R 2), root mean square error (RMSE), absolute average deviation (AAD), were calculated for the comparison of estimation capabilities of RSM and ANN. The R 2 was calculated using Eq. (2):
(2)
R
2
= 1 -
∑
i = 1
n
(
Y
pre
-
Y
exp
)
2
∑
i = 1
n

Y
m
-
Y
exp
)
2
where Y pre is the predicted resveratrol extraction yield (by either RSM or ANN), Y exp is the observed resveratrol extraction yield, Y m is the average resveratrol extraction yield, and n is the number of experiments (n =27 for CCRD experiments and n =12 for external experiments).

The RMSE was calculated using Eq. (3):
(3)
RMSE =
∑
i = 1

)
2
n
where Y pre and Y exp are the predicted and experimental response, respectively, and n is the number of experiments (n =12 for external experiments).

The AAD was calculated using Eq. (4):
(4)
AAD =
∑

Y

)
n
× 100
where Y exp is the experimental response, Y pre is the predicted response responses, and n is the number of experiments (n =12 for external experiments).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Efficacy of different enzymes on extraction yield of resveratrol
ce:section.ce:para: In present study, the ultrasonic extraction of resveratrol in the absence or presence of glycosidase treatment was further examined. According to Fig. 1A and B, the extraction yield of resveratrol in glycosidase-treated group was significantly higher than that in non-treated group. Following, two type of commercially available glycosidases, Pectinex® and Viscozyme® were screened for their efficiency on the extraction of resveratrol form P. cuspidatum. As shown in Fig.1C, the resveratrol only yielded 3.76mg/g in non-enzymatic extraction, while the resveratrol yields significantly increased (nearly 2–3-fold) when application of Pectinex® or Viscozyme® in the extraction. The amounts of resveratrol extracted with Pectinex® (10.38mg/g) were slightly higher than that extracted with Viscozyme® (8.89mg/g).

The plant cell walls mainly contain cellulose, hemicelluloses and pectin. Cellulose appears to a key component of the primary cell wall structure of P. cuspidatum, whereas pectin seems to be the major component in the secondary cell wall structure and acts as an adhesive between cells [24]. Pectinex® derived from Aspergillus aculeatus contains several enzymes including pectinase, cellulase, β-galactosidase, polygalacturonanase and fructosyltransferase, and the prominent one is polygalacturonanase, which can effectively degrade pectin [25]. A report showed that polygalacturonase has higher improving effect in the extraction of luteolin and apigenin form pigeinpea leaves than β-glucosidase, probably due to its better performance in cell degradation activities [26]. Therefore, increased degradation of cell walls within P. cuspidatum may be the major cause of higher extraction yields of resveratrol in response to Pectinex® treatment, because Pectinex® can efficiently promote the release of resveratrol and piceid through degradation of the cells of P. cuspidatum during ultrasonic extraction. Simultaneously, the piceid will be rapidly hydrolyzed by Pectinex®-catalyzed deglucosidation and turn into the resveratrol. Overall, Pectinex® was more efficient for the extraction of resveratrol when compared to Viscozyme® and was applied to the enzyme-assisted ultrasonic extraction in this study.

The main objective of this work was to develop and evaluate an approach to better understand the relationship between the variables and the extraction yield. There are several factors that affect the process of enzyme-assisted ultrasonic extraction of resveratrol, including enzyme amount and reaction conditions (e.g. temperature, time and pH value). To improve extraction yield and reaction rate, optimization of the process is often required [27]. In comparison with the one-factor-at-a-time design, RSM, as employed in this study, is more efficient in reducing the experimental runs and time for determination of optimal conditions. The experimental conditions and the response values from the experimental design are listed in Table 2, which shows the highest extraction yield (11.7±0.16mg) at reaction temperature of 55°C, reaction time of 5h, enzyme amount of 711 and pH value of 6 (run no. 4), as well as the lowest extraction yield (1.71±0.09mg) at reaction temperature of 65°C, reaction time of 7h, enzyme amount of 553 and pH value of 7.5 (run no. 2). The manipulated and response variables were analyzed to fit a regression model. The full quadratic second-order model obtained by multiple regression analysis of the experimental data (actual values) by applying RSM was expressed in Eq. (5). The equation model was used for the future prediction of resveratrol yield.
(5)
Y = - 85.18606 + 2.17438
X
1
+ 3.99982
X
2
+ 0.034773
X
3
+ 8.49152
X
4
- 0.019701
X
1
X
2
- 0.0000222972
X
1
X
3
+ 0.00375848
X
1
X
4
- 0.0000368657
X
2
X
3
- 0.084083
X
2
X
4
- 0.00124770
X
3
X
4
- 0.019711
X
1
2
- 0.22035
X
2
2
- 0.0000286310
X
3
2
- 0.76998
X
4
2
where Y is the resveratrol amount and X 1, X 2, X 3 and X 4 are temperature, reaction time, enzyme amount and pH, respectively. This second-order model was highly significant and adequate to represent the actual relationship between the response (resveratrol yield) and the experimental variables according to a very small P value (<0.01) determined by the analysis of variance (ANOVA). Besides, the significance of each variable on the response was also determined by ANOVA, and the order was described as follows: X 4 (P =0.0002)> X 1 2 (P =0.0006)> X 4 2 (P =0.0016)> X 2 2 (P =0.0623)> X 1 (P =0.0745)> X 3 2 (P =0.1169)> X 3 (P =0.2308)> X 2 (P =0.3548)> X 1 X 2 (P =0.4398)> X 3 X 4 (P =0.5587)> X 2 X 4 (P =0.6191)> X 1 X 4 (P =0.9102)> X 1 X 3 (P =0.9456)> X 1 X 3 (P =0.9792).

The ANN structure consisting of four neurons (temperature, reaction time, enzyme amount and pH value) in the input layer, several neurons in the hidden layer and one neuron (resveratrol yield) in the output layer was proposed in present study. The best ANN model were optimally determined from several CCRD data-employing networks based on the mean squared errors (MSE) that were statistically calculated from the actual values and prediction values of each network [28]. These networks varied with the number of neurons in the hidden layer, learning algorithms, transfer functions and iteration. According to the results, the hidden layer with 15 neurons, the learning algorithm (batch back propagation, BBP), the transfer functions of hidden layer (sigmoid) and output layer (hyperbolic tangent, Tanh), and the iteration (10,000 runs) were determined, and the best ANN model with a 4-15-1 topology was finally established. Table 2 indicates that the extraction yield predicted by ANN were different from those predicted by RSM. In terms of the experimental runs with highest and lowest response values, the ANN-predicted values (11.96mg and 1.86mg) were very close to the actual yields (11.7mg and 1.71mg), yet the yields were far to the values predicted by RSM.

According to Fig. 2, the response surface plots obtained from ANN indicate the effect of the experimental variables and their mutual interaction on resveratrol yield. Fig.2 A indicates the effects of the reaction time and reaction temperature on the resveratrol yield, at a constant enzyme amount (395PGNU) and pH value (6.0). Fig.2B indicates the effects of reaction time and enzyme amount on the resveratrol yield at a constant reaction temperature (55°C) and pH value (6.0). Fig.2C indicates the effects of reaction time and pH value on the resveratrol yield at a constant reaction temperature (55°C) and enzyme amount (395PGNU). These results indicated that enzyme amount had no significant effect on the yield. Increase in enzyme amount may result in increased mixture viscosity, decreased mutual solubility and inhibition of substrate diffusion, thus leading to reducing mass transfer and unfavorable interactions between enzyme particles and substrates. Notably, the reaction temperature and the pH level exhibited significant effect on the yield, which rapidly increased until the temperature reached 50°C and the pH reached 6.0, respectively. According to these response surface plots, a maximum resveratrol yield of 11.88mg/g was also determined under the reaction condition that pH of 5.4, temperature of 55°C, enzyme amount of 395PGNU and reaction time of 5h.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparison of models developed by RSM and ANN
ce:section.ce:para: The predicted values using the selected model of RSM and network of ANN for the experimental runs and the actual values of resveratrol yields are provided in Table 2. The difference between the RSM and ANN was prominent in the experiments at the edge points such as in experiments 1, 3, 4, 14, 16, 17, 18 and 27, whereas at the center points the predicted levels by RSM and ANN were similar. Fig. 3 represents the correlation between the actual values and predicted values of both ANN and RSM, and the results showed that ANN-predicted values were more closed to the actual values, suggesting that ANN was better than RSM in data fitting.

In addition to CCRD experiments, external experiments were performed to validate the predictive abilities of RSM and ANN as presented in Table 3 . The results clearly indicated that the ANN model provided more accurate prediction compared to the RSM model. RSM and ANN were further statistically compared on the basis of R 2, AAD and RMSE calculated from the validation data (Table 4 ). The ANN model showed higher values of R 2 (0.8474) than those obtained by RSM (0.3922). On the other hand, the values of AAD (30.5765) and RMSE (1.8473) for ANN were lower than those obtained by RSM (106.2083 and 5.4929, respectively). As a similar result, the ANN model is much more accurate in prediction as compared to the RSM by modeling of curdlan production from Paenibacillus polymyxa [29]. The results indicated that ANN was superior to RSM in the predictive capability of resveratrol production assisted by Pectinex®.

In this study, Pectinex®-assisted ultrasonic extraction of resveratrol from P. cuspidatum is successfully developed, which is modeled using RSM and ANN. In validation test, the constructed ANN shows larger R 2 values, whereas AAD and RMSE values of which are smaller as compared to those observed from the proposed RSM, suggesting that ANN displays better predictive ability. Optimization of the extraction process by ANN indicates that the maximum resveratrol yield was 11.88mg/g at pH value of 5.4, temperature of 55°C, enzyme amount of 395PGNU and reaction time of 5h. In conclusion, enzyme-assisted ultrasonic extraction significantly increases the resveratrol yield from P. cuspidatum, and this approach can be further applied for commercial use.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was funded by the Ministry of Science and Technology of Taiwan with grant number of MOST-101-2313-B-005-040-MY3.
